This theorem can be useful in showing that a number of familiar properties

[[strong]]Property 1:[[/strong]] If a series of complex numbers converges, the @@math:78:646b4f@@-th term converges to

Another important property of series of complex numbers that follows from a corresponding property in

                    convergence
                    of that series.

Code

@@text:93:192ff5@@

#Complex number
Z = 0.72 + ί * 0.61

#Circle of radius 1
c = Circle((0,0), 1)

#Number of terms of the partial series
n = Slider(0, 250, 1, 1, 150, false, true, false, false)
SetValue(n, 250)

#Define the sequence z^n
S = Join({0 + ί * 0, 1 + ί * 0}, Sequence(Z^j, j, 1, n))

#Define partial sum
SP = Sequence(Sum(S, j), j, 1, n + 2)

#Finally join the points of the partial sum
L = Sequence(Segment(Element(SP, j), Element(SP, j + 1)), j, 1, n + 1)

This theorem can be useful in showing that a number of familiar properties of series in calculus carry over to series whose terms are complex numbers.

Property 1: If a series of complex numbers converges, the @@math:78:646b4f@@-th term converges to zero as @@math:79:df562c@@ tends to infinity.

It follows from Property 1 that the terms of convergent series are bounded. That is, when series (\ref{series01}) converges, there exists a positive constant @@math:80:82737d@@ such that @@math:26:dec25a@@

Another important property of series of complex numbers that follows from a corresponding property in calculus is the following.

Property 2: The absolute convergence of a series of complex numbers implies the convergence of that series.

Recall that series (\ref{series01}) is said to be absolutely convergent if the series @@math:20:623aa5@@ of real numbers @@math:81:25fd81@@ converges.

Geometric series exploration

The series introduced in the previous example @@math:24:f68259@@ is known as the geometric series.

Use the following applet to explore this series. Drag the point @@math:91:e85fa0@@ around. Observe what happens when it is inside, outside or on the border of the unit circle. Drag the slider to show the partial sum.

Code

Enter the following script in GeoGebra to explore it yourself and make your own version. The symbol # indicates comments.

#Complex number
Z = 0.72 + ί * 0.61

#Circle of radius 1
c = Circle((0,0), 1)

#Number of terms of the partial series
n = Slider(0, 250, 1, 1, 150, false, true, false, false)
SetValue(n, 250)

#Define the sequence z^n
S = Join({0 + ί * 0, 1 + ί * 0}, Sequence(Z^j, j, 1, n))

#Define partial sum
SP = Sequence(Sum(S, j), j, 1, n + 2)

#Finally join the points of the partial sum
L = Sequence(Segment(Element(SP, j), Element(SP, j + 1)), j, 1, n + 1)
